coredata.dc:title: Synergistic optical enhancement of Au/AgNTs@CDs immunoprobe with tips and gaps for SERS/FL dual mode detection of PSA
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Yan-ling
Zhu, Jian
Weng, Guo-jun
Li, Jian-jun
Zhao, Jun-wu
coredata.dc:description: Robust and highly sensitive nano-optical immunoprobes have emerged as powerful tools to improve the accuracy and sensitivity of early disease diagnosis. This work develops a pentagonal gold/silver nanotube @ carbon dot (Au/AgNTs@CDs) SERS/FL dual-mode immunoprobe for detecting prostate-specific antigen (PSA) in serum by subtly integrating small gaps and branch tip structures. Benefiting from the synergistic optical enhancement of tips and small gaps, this novel rough porous Au/Ag nanotube exhibits larger specific surface area and more active sites as well as remarkable stability. The optical enhancement brought by the innovative structural design increases the detection sensitivity of the immunoprobe to the state of the art. To the best of our knowledge, the limit of detection is an order of magnitude lower than reported in the literature in SERS and fluorescence modes, respectively. Furthermore, both modes exhibit excellent robustness and specificity. Therefore, the design of this immunoprobe provides a new perspective and broad application prospects for improving the sensitivity and accuracy of clinical diagnosis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Gold/silver nanotubes
SERS
Fluorescence
Dual-mode
Carbon dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of Au/AgNT@CDs SERS/FL dual-mode Immunoprobe
Optical properties and morphology characterization of CDs
The SERS and FL characteristics of Au/AgNTs@CDs probes
Stability and accuracy of Au/AgNTs@CDs SERS/FL dual mode immunoprobe
SERS/FL dual-mode method for detecting serum samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Absorption spectra, Ex (maximum excitation spectra), and Em (emission spectra) of CDs, the inset shows digital photos of CDs solution and control aqueous solution. (b) The high-resolution TEM of CDs; (b1) The inset shows diameter distribution and (b2) lattice spacing of CDs; (c) Raman spectra and (d) FT-IR spectra of CDs.

The FL emission spectra and the FL intensity as the increasing volume of HAuCl4 of Au/AgNTs prepared with two different surfactants (a-b) CTAB and (c-d) PVP. The solid line “——” indicates the presence of antigen (Au/AgNTs@CDs) in the sample, and the dashed line “.” indicates no antigen (Au/AgNTs+CDs) in the sample.
The SERS spectra of Au/AgNTs@CDs probes prepared by two different surfactants, and the Raman intensity (1589 cm−1) with the different volume of HAuCl4: (a-b) CTAB and (c-d) PVP.

(a) UV–vis spectra of Au/AgNTs at different days; (b) FL emission spectrum of Au/AgNTs at different days; SERS (c) spectrum and (d) intensity (at 1589 cm−1) of Au/AgNTs at different days; (e) TEM images of Au/AgNTs stored at 4 ℃ for 0 and 30 days; (f) Raman spectra and (g) intensities of CDs from randomly selected 15 positions of Au/AgNTs substrates. (h) SERS intensity distribution mapping (40 × 40 μm2) at 1589 cm−1 of CDs adsorbed on the Au/AgNTs substrates.

(a) FL spectra of the Ag/AuNTs@CDs immunoprobe with different concentrations of PSA; (b) calibration curve between the fluorescence intensity and the PSA concentration; (c) SERS spectra of the Ag/AuNTs@CDs immunoprobe with different concentrations of PSA; (d) calibration curve between the Raman intensity and the PSA concentration; Selectivity experiments in (e) FL and (f) SERS modes, respectively. The concentration of PSA is 10 pg/mL, while the concentrations of CEA, CA125, AFP, BSA, IgG, Glu, AA, DA and HP are 100 ng/mL, respectively.

(a) The preparation of rough hollow porous Au/AgNTs; (b) The preparation of carbon dots; (c) The highly rough porous Au/AgNTs@CDs SERS/FL dual-mode immunosensor for the ultrasensitive determination of PSA.
Comparison of EF for several different noble metal nanoparticle SERS substrates.
Detection of PSA in serum based on rough porous Au/AgNTs@CDs immunoprobe.

head.ce:title: Synergistic optical enhancement of Au/AgNTs@CDs immunoprobe with tips and gaps for SERS/FL dual mode detection of PSA

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The Key Laboratory of Biomedical Information Engineering of Ministry of Education, School of Life Science and Technology, Xi’an Jiaotong University, Xi’an 710049, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Key Laboratory of Biomedical Information Engineering of Ministry of Education, School of Life Science and Technology, Xi’an Jiaotong University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: The Key Laboratory of Biomedical Information Engineering of Ministry of Education, School of Life Science and Technology, Xi’an Jiaotong University, Xi’an 710049, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: School of Life Science and Technology, Xi’an Jiaotong University, Xi’an 710049, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Life Science and Technology, Xi’an Jiaotong University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Robust and highly sensitive nano-optical immunoprobes have emerged as powerful tools to improve the accuracy and sensitivity of early disease diagnosis. This work develops a pentagonal gold/silver nanotube @ carbon dot (Au/AgNTs@CDs) SERS/FL dual-mode immunoprobe for detecting prostate-specific antigen (PSA) in serum by subtly integrating small gaps and branch tip structures. Benefiting from the synergistic optical enhancement of tips and small gaps, this novel rough porous Au/Ag nanotube exhibits larger specific surface area and more active sites as well as remarkable stability. The optical enhancement brought by the innovative structural design increases the detection sensitivity of the immunoprobe to the state of the art. To the best of our knowledge, the limit of detection is an order of magnitude lower than reported in the literature in SERS and fluorescence modes, respectively. Furthermore, both modes exhibit excellent robustness and specificity. Therefore, the design of this immunoprobe provides a new perspective and broad application prospects for improving the sensitivity and accuracy of clinical diagnosis.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Prostate cancer (PCa) is a common malignant tumor, with approximately 375,000 deaths worldwide each year [1]. Currently, prostate specific antigen (PSA) is widely acknowledged as the most effective serum biomarker for clinical PCa detection[2]. Serum concentrations of PSA higher than 4 ng/mL are typically indicative of PCa or other prostate diseases[3]. A variety of methods for the determination of PSA have been developed, such as electrochemistry[4], surface plasmon resonance-based biosensors [5], and colorimetric immunoassay[6]. Nevertheless, these techniques still suffer from unsatisfactory detection sensitivity and poor reproducibility in practical applications. Therefore, there is an urgent need to develop more sensitive early diagnostic tools for prostate cancer.

In recent years, fluorescent (FL) biosensors have become one of the mainstream methods for biochemical detection due to their high sensitivity and fast readout speed[7]. Although metal-enhanced fluorescence (MEF) occurring between fluorophores and metal nanomaterials can enhance the quantum efficiency and photostability of fluorophores, most fluorescence sensors still have photobleaching and autofluorescence issues[8]. Surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) has significant advantages in resisting photobleaching, low autofluorescence, and rich fingerprint information compared with fluorescence[9]. Benefiting from localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR), the electromagnetic field and SERS signal of the metal surface substrate can be greatly amplified, and trace amounts of molecules can be detected through Raman scattering[10]. In addition, this single-mode signal output is easily affected by background fluctuations in complex physiological environments, resulting in poor reliability and low repeatability[11]. Therefore, the combination of FL and SERS dual modes is an optimal strategy with complementary advantages, which can provide multi-scale information and reduce false positive results, thereby achieving accurate and reliable PSA detection

The key process to achieve highly sensitive analysis of PSA lies in the preparation of nanostructures with significant optical enhancement and excellent stability for SERS/FL dual-mode detection. Noble metallic nanomaterials possess unique LSPR and electromagnetic field enhancement effects, which can significantly enhance the optical signals of target molecules. The SERS and FL properties are closely related to the composition, shape, size, and surface roughness of noble metal nanomaterials [12]. First, the stability of the material is important. Silver nanomaterials exhibit superior enhancement effects in terms of LSPR and SERS compared to gold nanomaterials. Nevertheless, silver nanomaterials are prone to oxidation, which severely limits their applications. Gold nanomaterials have good biocompatibility and excellent chemical stability. By combining the advantages of both, Au/Ag alloy nanomaterials can increase the stability of the immunoprobe [13]. Furthermore, the spatially extended electromagnetic field facilitates the adsorption of more signal molecules, providing higher optical enhancement and improving detection sensitivity. such as irregular gold nanoparticles [14] and hollow porous nanocages [15], have been utilized in the preparation of SERS/FL dual-mode immunoprobes. These findings have demonstrated that the sharpness, roughness and density of hot spots play an important role in enhancing the detection sensitivity of SERS/FL dual-mode immunoprobes[16]. Taking inspiration from this, our work integrates branch tips and gaps into a singular nanomaterial, synthesizing rough porous pentagonal Au/Ag alloy nanotubes. This novel nanostructure not only increases the specific surface area, allowing for enhanced adsorption of signal molecules, but also benefits from the synergistic optical enhancement effect of branch tips and gap structures, significantly improving the detection sensitivity of PSA.

Carbon dots (CDs) are a new type of fluorescent nanomaterial with a particle diameter of less than 10 nm [17]. Due to their low preparation cost, excellent chemical stability, favorable electronic conductivity, biocompatibility, and low toxicity, they have wide applications in the field of biosensing [18]. In this study, CDs as fluorescent and Raman dual-signal molecules were used to develop an Au/AgNTs@CDs dual-mode high-sensitivity immunoprobe for the specific detection of PSA, as shown in scheme 1. The rough porous Au/AgNTs synthesized using polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) exhibit a larger specific surface area, offering more active sites for the analytes compared to those prepared with cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB). Meanwhile, the presence of branch tips structures and small gaps configuration induces high-density hot spots, enhancing the optical characteristics of Au/AgNTs@CDs immunoprobe. The design of this novel immunoprobe offers a new perspective for improving the sensitivity and accuracy of clinical diagnosis.
In the Supplementary data, the following are provided in detail: Chemicals and Materials (S1), Characterization Equipment (S2).

AgNWs: the silver nanowires were synthesized according to a previous report[12], the main preparation steps are as follows: 250 mL of ethylene glycol (EG) solution in an oil bath was heated at 175 °C with stirring. The temperature and stirring are maintained during the synthesis. Then 3.0 g of PVP was added to the solution. After PVP was dissolved, 200 μL of NaCl ethylene glycol solution was added. After waiting for 5 min, 10 mL of silver nitrate was added. Finally, stop the reaction when the solution turns pale. The product was washed once with ethanol and five times with deionized water.

Au/AgNTs: the above-synthesized AgNWs solution will be centrifuged to remove the supernatant, then add 4 mL PVP solution or CTAB solution, and sonicate for 15 min. Then add different volumes of HAuCl4 solution for reaction at 80 °C for 10 min. Then the above solution was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min, washed twice with 3 mL of deionized water, and finally dispersed in water.

CDs: The preparation of CDs referred to and improved the literature method[19]: Anhydrous citric acid (1.0 g) and carbamide (5.0 g) were dissolved in deionized water. And the solution was transferred to a polytetrafluoroethylene autoclave, which was heated at 180 ℃ for 6 h. After cooling to room temperature, the solution was dialyzed for 12 h. The dialysate was concentrated to 50 mL by a rotary evaporator, and the concentrated solution was freeze-dried to obtain the powder products. The powder was stored in a dry and dark environment for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Au/AgNT@CDs SERS/FL dual-mode Immunoprobe
ce:section.ce:para: A typical immunoassay procedure is as follows: Au/AgNTs solution conjugated to antibody Pa3 and different concentrations of PSA are mixed and incubated, then Pa5-CDs solution is added, purified by centrifugation, and redispersed in PBS solution. Then take 100 μL of the mixture and use it to record the FL intensity with a FL spectrometer, and take 10 μL of the mixture and drop it on the quartz glass for the detection of Raman intensity. In addition, in order to investigate the anti-interference ability of the detection method, interfering substance (carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA), carbohydrate antigen 125 (CA125), immunoglobulin G (IgG), bovine serum albumin (BSA), alpha fetoprotein (AFP), glucose (Glu), hydrogen peroxide (HP), ascorbic acid (AA) and dopamine (DA) at 100 ng/mL) were added to the Au/AgNTs solution of conjugated antibodies, and the unlinked proteins were removed by centrifugation. protein, resuspended in PBS solution. Then add Pa5-CDs solution, incubate for a period of time, centrifuge to remove unbound excess antibody, resuspend in PBS solution, and carry out the next step of SERS/FL detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties and morphology characterization of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The UV–visible absorption peak of the CDs at 337 nm is shown in Fig. 1a. Under 352 nm excitation, the CDs exhibit the strongest FL emission at 450 nm. The inset confirms that the CDs emit blue fluorescence, which is consistent with previous findings in the literature[18]. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) characterization of the CDs revealed that they were spherical in shape with well dispersion and had an average diameter of 6.50 nm (Fig. 1b). Moreover, the lattice spacing of the CDs was found to be 0.21 nm (Fig. 1b2), indicating alignment with others commonly observed in CDs [19]. The Raman spectrum (Fig. 1c) of CDs displays two peaks at 1357 cm−1 and 1589 cm−1, which correspond to in-plane stretching vibrations of sp3-hybridized carbon atoms and sp2-hybridized carbon atoms, respectively [18]. The surface functional groups of the CDs were identified using FT-IR spectroscopy (Fig. 1d). The broad peak observed in the range of 3408–3246 cm−1can be attributed to v (-OH) and v (-NH). The absorption band at 2817 cm−1 indicated v (C-H). The absorption band at 1737 cm−1 was assigned to v (CO). Moreover, the absorption bands at 1485 cm−1 and 1413 cm−1were associated with the v (C-OH), while the absorption bands at 1179 cm−1 and 1072 cm−1 were attributed to the v (C-N) and the β (C-H)[20]. These spectra findings indicate that the CDs synthesized in this experiment possess various functional groups, including hydroxyl, carboxyl, amino, and amide bonds.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The SERS and FL characteristics of Au/AgNTs@CDs probes
ce:section.ce:para: The strong attachment between nanoparticles and antibodies can be achieved by functionalizing of nanoparticles with reactive groups. These modified nanoparticles can undergo acylation reactions with the amino or carboxyl groups present on the amino acid side chains of the antibody surfaces. Detailed procedures regarding the modification of Au/AgNTs-Ab1 and CDs-Ab2 are outlined in Supplementary Material S4. Surface modification of Au/AgNTs is accomplished using thioglycolic acid (TGA) to form Au-S bonds, facilitating specific antibody binding while minimizing nonspecific adsorption of other biomolecules onto the nanoparticles. Fig. S12 in Supplementary Material demonstrates a 12 nm red-shift in the absorption spectrum of TGA-modified nanoparticles, likely attributed to the alteration of the dielectric environment resulting from Au-S bond formation[21]. To complete the antibody coupling process, the appropriate volume of prostate antibody solution is added to the system, followed by refrigeration at 4 °C for 1 h. Subsequently, 1.0 wt% BSA is introduced to block unbound sites, and the system is rinsed with PBS. Post-re-suspension, a 24 nm red-shift in the absorption spectrum confirms the successful modification of the antibody. The inclusion of BSA solution in the experiment serves to further prevent nonspecific binding, decrease nonspecific adsorption, and enhance the precision and reproducibility of the experimental outcomes.

Initially, the CDs were diluted in ultrapure water, CTAB solution (0.02 M) and PVP solution (10 mg/mL) used in this experiment, respectively. And it was observed that the FL emission spectra intensity did not show significant changes. Therefore, the influence of surfactants on the FL intensity of CDs was excluded during the experiment. The Au/AgNTs@CDs showed maximum excitation and emission wavelength at 352 nm and 450 nm, respectively. Au/AgNTs-Ab1 displayed a broad transverse LSPR peak within the range of 300 nm ∼ 950 nm. There is a significant spectral overlap between the two, precisely meeting the basic conditions for fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). The fluorescence intensity is dependent on the distance between metal nanoparticles and fluorescent molecules. When the distance (d) between metal nanoparticles and fluorescent molecules is less than 5 nm, a phenomenon known as FRET occurs[22]. In this process, the fluorescent molecules transfer energy to the metal nanoparticles through a non-radiative path. This energy transfer results in the fluorescent molecules returning to their ground state, consequently leading to the quenching of the fluorescence signal. The fluorescence signal is enhanced when an excited fluorophore is positioned near metals at a distance of 5 ∼ 20 nm. This is due to the unique LSPR effect of metal nanoparticles, which significantly amplifies the electric field surrounding the nanoparticles. This activation enhances the excitation efficiency of fluorescent molecules near the surface of the nanoparticles, ultimately boosting the intensity of fluorescence emission[23].

The FL emission spectra and FL intensity of Au/AgNTs-Ab1 and CDs-Ab2 systems prepared with two different surfactants with and without antigen (Au/AgNTs@CDs, solid line “—”) and without antigen (Au/AgNTs+CDs, dashed line “----”), respectively, is shown in Fig. 2. It can be observed that the FL intensity significantly increases when antigen is added compared to samples without antigen (∆F represents the difference in FL intensity before and after the addition of antigen). This is primarily due to the short distance between CDs and Au/AgNTs in the absence of antigen, which leads to the transfer of energy from CDs (as the FL donor) to the Au/AgNTs via nonradiative processes, resulting in a weakened FL signal. However, the addition of antigen leads to the formation of an Au/AgNTs-Ab1-PSA-Ab2-CDs complex, increasing the distance between CDs and Au/AgNTs and subsequently restoring the FL signal. Notably, when the volume of HAuCl4 is 1600 μL, the FL intensity (Au/AgNTs@CDs) is restored to 1.5 times that of the sample without antigen (Au/AgNTs+CDs). This may be attributed to the increased porous nanostructure of Au/AgNTs by CTAB, which provides more binding sites for CDs and results in the maximum increase in FL intensity compared to other states of Au/AgNTs[21]. In Fig. 2(c-d), it is shown that when PVP is used as the surfactant and the volume of HAuCl4 is 1600 μL, the maximum FL intensity (Au/AgNTs@CDs) is restored to 3.13 times that of the sample without antigen (Au/AgNTs+CDs). The reason may be that there are a lot of small gaps and the longest branch tips in Au/AgNTs when the volume of HAuCl4 is 1600 μL. The porous nanostructure and branch tips provide more binding sites for the FL signal molecules (CDs), resulting in a greater increase in FL intensity. In addition, the branch tips and smaller pores Au/AgNTs prepared by PVP were able to produce stronger localized electric fields compared with the hollow porous Au/AgNTs prepared by CTAB when the volume of HAuCl4 was 1600 μL[21]. A stronger localized electric field can significantly increase the excitation efficiency of fluorescent molecules near the nanoparticle surface, which ultimately enhances the intensity of fluorescence emission[24].Therefore, we believe that the branch tips and smaller pores Au/AgNTs@CDs immunosensor prepared by PVP may have higher detection sensitivity in fluorescence detection than the hollow and porous Au/AgNTs@CDs immunosensor prepared by CTAB.

Fig. 3(a-b) display the SERS spectra and Raman intensity of Au/AgNTs@CDs probe prepared by CTAB. It’s revealed that the Raman intensity of Au/AgNTs@CDs immunoprobe shows a trend of first increasing and then decreasing as the volume of HAuCl4 increases (400–3200 μL). The Raman intensity reaches its maximum when the volume of HAuCl4 is 1600 μL. The porous structure provides more hotspots, significantly enhancing the electromagnetic field intensity, hence the pentagonal porous Au/AgNTs exhibit excellent Raman activity. In order to quantitatively analyze the SERS activity of Au/AgNTs, the enhancement factor (EF) was calculated based on the normal Raman signals of the CDs solution at a concentration of 2 mg/mL and the SERS Raman signals of CDs at a concentration of 5 × 10−6 mg/mL with the following formula[12,25]:
E F =
(
I
S E R S
/
C
S E R S
)
/
(
I
b u l k
/
C
b u l k
)

Here, ISERS denotes the enhanced intensity of CDs adsorbed on the surface of the nanoparticle, IBulk denotes the normal Raman intensity of CDs in bulk solution, CBulk denotes the concentration of CDs in bulk solution, and CSERS denotes the concentration of CDs in SERS spectrum. The result showed that the maximum EF of Au/AgNTs prepared by CTAB is 5.6 × 107. Fig. 3(c-d) display the SERS spectra and Raman intensity of Au/AgNTs@CDs probe prepared by PVP. It can be observed that the probe prepared with PVP has stronger Raman activity compared with CTAB. The difference in Raman intensity of the two is nearly double when the volume of HAuCl4 reaches 1600 μL, and the maximum EF reaches 1.5 × 108. Compared with the EF of other nanoparticles reported in the literature in Table 1, the rough hollow porous pentagonal Au/AgNTs prepared by PVP have a significant advantage. This is mainly because the tip effect of the branched structure distributed on the surface of the Au/AgNTs further enhances the electric field intensity in addition to the smaller pore structure providing a stronger local electric field[26]. This dual enhancement effect gives the rough porous Au/AgNTs a significant advantage in detecting low abundance signals. Therefore, we believe that the rough porous Au/AgNTs@CDs immunosensor prepared by PVP may have higher detection sensitivity in Raman detection than the hollow porous Au/AgNTs@CDs immunosensor prepared by CTAB.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability and accuracy of Au/AgNTs@CDs SERS/FL dual mode immunoprobe
ce:section.ce:para: The stability of the immunoprobe is very important for its practical applications. Initially, seven Au/AgNTs solutions were prepared in parallel and stored them at 4 ℃. The absorption spectra of the solution were detected at 0, 7, 14, 21, 28, and 35 days, respectively. As depicted in Fig. 4a, the absorption peak intensity of the Au/AgNTs remained almost unchanged at 28 days, and the intensity of the absorption peak at 35 days was 93.4% of the initial intensity. It’s indicated that the Au/AgNTs prepared in this experiment are stable for at least one month. Furthermore, the solution was used to prepare Au/AgNTs@CDs immunoprobe. Fig. 4b shows that the FL intensity of the immunoprobe at 35 days was 91.8% of the initial intensity. In addition, the characteristic peaks of the Raman spectrum of the Au/AgNTs@CDs immunoprobe remained similar after 35 days of storage, and the Raman intensity still exceeded 70%, showing good stability (Fig. 4c and d). Furthermore, the morphological features of the branch tips and small gaps on the Au/AgNTs surface remained intact after for 0 and 30 days of storage in Fig. 4e. In summary, those results indicate that the Au/AgNTs exhibit good stability within one month.

In order to test the uniformity of substrate, 15 points were randomly selected on the same substrate for detection. It can be seen in Fig. 4f that these Raman spectra have similar peak shapes, and the characteristic peaks of CDs are significantly enhanced[31,32]. Fig. 4g shows that the relative standard deviation (RSD) of SERS intensity of these spectra at 1589 cm−1 is 4.8%, which indicates the good uniformity of the Au/AgNTs substrate. Moreover, the 40 × 40 μm2 area on the substrate was randomly selected for Raman imaging. As shown in Fig. 4h, the depth of the color represents the Raman intensity at 1589 cm−1. It can be seen that the color of the color block in the Raman imaging area is relatively uniform, and the highest intensity value is greater than twice the lowest value. The above results show that the immunoprobe has good stability and repeatability, which is suitable for actual sample detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: SERS/FL dual-mode method for detecting serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to prove the applicability of Au/AgNTs@CDs immunoprobe in actual sample detection. First, it can be seen from Fig. 5a that the intensity of FL emission spectrum gradually increases with the increase of PSA concentration. The relationship between the intensity of the FL emission spectrum and PSA concentration is shown in Fig. 5b. It can be seen that PSA has a good linear relationship in the range of 10−4∼102 ng/mL, and its limit of detection (LOD) reaches 0.03 pg/mL. Table S1 shows the performance of different nanomaterials in detecting PSA in SERS and FL modes, respectively. Compared with other FL methods reported in the literature[33–36], this work has a lower LOD and wider linear range. The large specific surface area of rough porous Au/AgNTs provides more adsorption sites for CDs. which results in the FL signals can be detected at lower concentrations. Similar result has also been reported in other literatures[37]. So the detection limit of Au/AgNTs@CDs immunoprobe in FL mode is one order of magnitude lower than that reported in the current literature. As can be seen from Fig. 5c, the SERS intensity detected by Au/AgNTs@CDs immunoprobe gradually increases with the increase of PSA concentration. The relationship between PSA concentration and SERS intensity detected by Au/AgNTs@CDs immunoprobe in Fig. 5d. It can be identified that PSA has a good linear relationship in the range of 10−3∼102 ng/mL, and the calculated LOD reaches 0.3 pg/mL. As can be seen from Table S1, compared with other SERS substrates, the Au/AgNTs@CDs immunoprobe prepared in the experiment also has a wider detection range and a lower LOD in SERS mode. This may be because the Au/AgNTs@CDs immunoprobe not only has a larger specific surface area but also provides more adsorption sites for signal molecules, so that Raman signals can still be detected at a lower concentration. Moreover, the tip effect of Au/AgNTs surface branch tips and the small gaps can generate more high-density hot spots, significantly increasing the surrounding electric field intensity. Therefore, the Au/AgNTs substrate can accurately identify the characteristic Raman peaks of target molecules even in low-concentration samples. To sum up, the Au/AgNTs@CDs immunoprobe prepared in this work exhibits low LOD and wide linear range in both FL and SERS modes. Moreover, the two modes can verify each other, effectively improves the accuracy of detection. Au/AgNTs@CDs SERS/FL dual-mode immunoprobe has good application prospects in actual sample detection.

To evaluate the specificity of Au/AgNTs@CDs immunoprobe in two different modes, we selected common biomolecules including carbohydrate antigen 125 (CA125), bovine serum albumin (BSA), alpha-fetoprotein (AFP), carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) and immunoglobulin (IgG), glucose (Glu), hydrogen peroxide (HP), ascorbic acid (AA) and dopamine (DA) as interfering substances in the experiment. First, we prepared ten identical serum samples, then added the above interferences at a concentration of 100 ng/mL, and PSA at a concentration of 10 pg/mL. Secondly, Au/AgNTs@CDs SERS/FL dual-mode immunoprobe was used to detect these samples. It can be seen from Fig. 5e that the FL intensity of the interfering protein is small. The SERS intensity of PSA detected by the Au/AgNTs@CDs immunoprobe was significantly higher than that of other interfering proteins (Fig. 5f). It demonstrates that the Au/AgNTs@CDs SERS/FL dual-mode immunoprobe for PSA detection has remarkable specificity. This can be primarily attributed to the utilization of a double antibody sandwich mode of antigen-antibody specific binding in the Au/AgNTs@CDs immunoprobe, and other antigens lack specific binding sites.

In order to investigate the reliability of Au/AgNTs@CDs dual-mode immunoprobe in practical applications, different concentrations of PSA were added to human serum for detection. Three parallel samples were prepared for each concentration, and the average value was taken as the final detection result. Table 2 shows the actual detection values and the corresponding added concentration. The recovery rate of PSA ranged from 93.9% to 108.0%, indicating the accuracy of the method was good. The RSD ranged from 1.47% to 15.5%. Overall, these results demonstrate that Au/AgNTs@CDs dual-mode immunoprobe prepared in this experiment meets the detection requirements of real samples.

In this work, we first investigated the effects of surfactants CTAB and PVP on the morphology and optical properties of Au/AgNTs, respectively. It was found that when CTAB was used as the surfactant, the morphology of the nanoparticles exhibited a changing pattern from partially hollow nanotubes to porous nanotubes, which were subsequently dealloyed into smaller fragments. The SERS activity was shown to increase and then decrease. Nevertheless, when PVP was used as the surfactant, the morphology of the nanoparticles underwent a transition from a partially hollow structure with surface branch tips to hollow porous nanotubes with the increased number of branch tips, which ultimately generated a core-shell linear structure. The SERS activity also showed a pattern of change with a first increase and then a decrease. Notably, the SERS activity of PVP-synthesized Au/AgNTs was significantly higher than that of CTAB-synthesized Au/AgNTs. The branch tips and small gaps Au/AgNTs synthesized by PVP synergistically enhanced the surrounding electric field and generate more hot spots. And the Au/AgNTs have a larger specific surface area, providing more adsorption sites for CDs. Therefore, we think that the Au/AgNTs@CDs SERS/FL dual-mode immunoprobe offers a highly sensitive and specific platform for PSA detection. The LOD of the immunoprobe in SERS and fluorescence modes were one order of magnitude lower than those reported in the literature, respectively. Furthermore, both modes exhibit excellent stability and anti-interference capabilities. The dual-mode strategy can provide complementary data to improve the reliability of PSA detection by cross-validating the results obtained by SERS and FL methods. In a word, the conditions of good stability, synergistic optical enhancement technology and high detection sensitivity support the feasibility of the Au/AgNTs@CDs SERS/FL dual-mode immunoprobe to be effectively used for the detection of PSA in clinical biological samples. The immunoprobe provides a highly sensitive and specific tool for the early detection of prostate cancer markers. This work not only proposes a new method to prepare rough porous Au/AgNTs, but also develops a highly sensitive SERS/FL dual-mode immunoprobe for PSA in serum. The above results indicate the great application potential of Au/AgNTs@CDs SERS/FL dual-mode immunoprobe in the field of biomedical detection.
Junwu Zhao: Resources. Jian-jun Li: Methodology. Guojun Weng: Methodology. Jian Zhu: Supervision. Yanling Liu: Writing – original draft.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: This paper is original and has been written by the stated authors who are all aware of its content and approve its submission. No conflict of interest exists. I hope that the reviewers find our work valuable and suitable for publication in your journal.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China under grant No. 11774283, Natural Science Basic Research Plan in Shaanxi Province of China under grant No. 2021JM-024 and Natural Science Basic Research Plan in Shaanxi Province of China under grant No. 2024JC-YBMS-526.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2024.135570.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R.L. Siegel, K.D. Miller, H.E. Fuchs, A. Jemal, Cancer statistics, 2022, CA-A Cancer Journal for Clinicians, 72(2022) 7-33.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Bioorthogonal chemistry-based high-efficient quantum dots binding boosts the detection sensitivity of plasmon-enhanced fluorescence platform for immunoassay

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N. Hui, J. Wang, D. Wang, P. Wang, X. Luo, S. Lv, An ultrasensitive biosensor for prostate specific antigen detection in complex serum based on functional signal amplifier and designed peptides with both antifouling and recognizing capabilities, Biosensors and Bioelectronics, 200(2022) 113921.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Yang, Y. Hu, N. Dong, G. Zhu, T. Zhu, N. Jiang, A novel SERS-based magnetic aptasensor for prostate specific antigen assay with high sensitivity, Biosensors and Bioelectronics, 94(2017) 286-291.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Ji, M. Lee, D. Kim, Detection of early stage prostate cancer by using a simple carbon nanotube@paper biosensor, Biosensors and Bioelectronics, 102(2018) 345-350.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A facile approach for significantly enhancing fluorescent gas sensing by oxygen plasma treatments

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Fu, J. Chen, H. Sun, K. Li, W. Xu, Q. He, et al., A facile approach for significantly enhancing fluorescent gas sensing by oxygen plasma treatments, Sensors and Actuators B: Chemical, 331(2021) 129397.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Yanling Liu received the Master’s degree in Beijing University of Chinese medicine, China, in 2019. Now she is a graduate student for a PhD degree in Xi’an Jiaotong University, China. Her research interests focus mainly on biomedical sensing.
Jian Zhu received his PhD degree in 2004 from Xi’an Jiaotong University, China. His research interests focus mainly on optical properties of nano-structured materials.
Guojun Weng received his PhD degree in 2013 from Xi’an Jiaotong University, China. His research interests focus mainly on biomedical sensing and photocatalysis based on plasmonic nanoparticles.
Jianjun Li received her PhD degree in 2003 from Xi’an Jiaotong University, China. Her research interests focus mainly on multi-information nanoprobe and bioseparation.
Junwu Zhao is a professor in the School of Life Science and Technology, Xi’an Jiaotong University, China. His research interests focus mainly on nano-biomaterials and device.
